items.title: Frappe Manager Deployer
items.text:
• Deploy in minutes

    Zero-downtime Frappe deployments with atomic releases, instant rollback, and automatic migrations. One command from development to production.

  • Atomic releases

    Every deployment creates a timestamped release. Switch instantly via symlinks. Keep N previous releases for instant rollback on failure.

  • :lucide-github:{ .lg .middle }   CI/CD ready

    Deploy automatically on push with GitHub Actions. Two strategies: pull (build on server) or ship (build in CI, rsync to remote).

  • Frappe Cloud sync

    Import app list, Python dependencies, and database backups directly from Frappe Cloud. Perfect for cloud-to-self-hosted migration.

  • Smart worker handling

    Workers drain gracefully before release switch. Maintenance mode only during migrations. Skip stale workers automatically.

  • Monorepo support

    Symlink subdirectory apps for efficient workspace management. Perfect for multi-app repositories with shared dependencies.

items.title: Install
items.text:
uv (recommended)pipxFrom source 
uv tool install frappe-deployer

pipx install frappe-deployer

git clone https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd.git
cd fmd
pip install -e .

items.title: Deploy your first site
items.text:
fmd deploy pull site.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode --backups

Your site deploys atomically — rollback instantly if anything fails.

Use a config file

# Create site.toml with your app list and settings
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

items.text:

Ready to deploy?

Head to the Guides to learn about deploy modes, GitHub Actions, hooks, Frappe Cloud sync, and more.

items.title: Building the Documentation
items.text: This guide explains how to build and preview the fmd documentation site locally.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Install development dependencies:

cd /path/to/fmd
uv sync --group dev

This installs: - zensical - MkDocs Material wrapper - mike - Documentation versioning - pytest and testing tools

Alternatively, install globally:

uv tool install zensical
uv tool install mike

Or use just commands (recommended):

# Install just
brew install just  # macOS
cargo install just  # Cross-platform via Rust

# View available commands
just

items.title: Development Server (Live Reload)
items.text:
Start a live-reloading development server for local editing:

just docs
# or
uv run zensical serve -a 127.0.0.1:8000

Then open http://localhost:8000 in your browser.

Changes to markdown files auto-reload. Note: This mode doesn't show the version selector.

items.title: Preview Versioned Docs Locally
items.text:
To preview with the version selector (requires deploying first):

# First deploy a version
just docs-deploy dev

# Then serve versioned docs
just docs-versioned
# or
uv run mike serve -F zensical.toml -a 127.0.0.1:8000

items.title: Build Static Site
items.text:
Build the static HTML site without deploying:

just docs-build
# or
uv run zensical build

Output goes to site/ directory.

items.title: Versioned Documentation (mike)
items.text: fmd uses mike to maintain multiple documentation versions.

items.title: Deploy Development Version
items.text:
Deploy current code as dev version:

just docs-deploy dev
# or
version=$(uv run python -c "from fmd.__about__ import __version__; print(__version__)")
uv run mike deploy dev --title "$version" -F zensical.toml

items.title: Deploy Release Version
items.text:
Deploy and mark as latest (for releases):

just docs-release
# or manually:
uv run mike deploy --push --update-aliases v1.0 latest --title "1.0.0" -F zensical.toml

This creates: - Version alias: v1.0 (major.minor) - Marks it as latest - Pushes to gh-pages branch

items.title: Set Default Version
items.text:
Set which version users see by default:

just docs-default latest
# or
uv run mike set-default --push latest -F zensical.toml

items.title: List Versions
items.text:
just docs-list
# or
uv run mike list -F zensical.toml

items.title: Delete a Version
items.text:
just docs-delete v0.9
# or
uv run mike delete v0.9 -F zensical.toml

items.title: Style Guide
items.text:
• Use bold for UI elements, commands, and emphasis
  • Use code for: commands, filenames, config keys, code snippets
  • Use admonitions for important notes:
  • !!! tip for helpful hints
  • !!! warning for cautions
  • !!! info for general information
  • !!! example for examples

items.title: Code Blocks
items.text:
Use fenced code blocks with language:

```bash
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml
```

```toml
site_name = "mysite.localhost"
```

items.title: Admonitions
items.text:
!!! tip "Optional Title"
    This is a helpful tip.

!!! warning
    This is a warning.

items.title: Tabbed Content
items.text:
=== "Tab 1"
    Content for tab 1

=== "Tab 2"
    Content for tab 2

items.title: Grid Cards
items.text:
<div class="grid cards" markdown>

-   :material-icon:{ .lg .middle }   **[Title](link.md)**

    ---

    Description text here.

</div>

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
Documentation is configured in zensical.toml:

  • Site metadata: name, description, URL
  • Navigation: menu structure
  • Theme: colors, features, icons
  • Social links: GitHub, PyPI

See the Zensical documentation for advanced configuration.

items.title: Automatic (via GitHub Actions)
items.text:
Documentation is automatically deployed when:

  1. Push to main branch
  2. GitHub Actions builds docs with mike
  3. Deploys to gh-pages branch

See .github/workflows/docs.yml for the workflow.

items.title: Manual Deployment
items.text:
For immediate deployment:

just docs-release

This: 1. Extracts version from fmd/__about__.py 2. Creates version slug (e.g., v1.0 from 1.0.0) 3. Deploys with aliases: v1.0 and latest 4. Pushes to gh-pages branch

items.title: Version Strategy
items.text:
• Development: Deploy as dev (doesn't affect version history)
  • Releases: Deploy as vX.Y + latest (e.g., v1.0, v1.1)
  • Default: Set latest as default redirect

Example workflow:

# During development
just docs-deploy dev

# On release v1.0.0
just docs-release  # Creates v1.0 + latest

# On release v1.1.0
just docs-release  # Creates v1.1 + latest (v1.0 still accessible)

items.title: Local Preview
items.text:
To preview the exact site that will be deployed:

zensical build
cd site
python -m http.server 8000

Open http://localhost:8000.

items.title: Version 0.1.0
items.text:
First public release of fmd, bringing production-grade deployment capabilities to Frappe Manager benches.

Key Features:

  • Atomic Releases: Every deployment creates a timestamped release, activated instantly via symlinks
  • Zero Downtime: Workers drain gracefully, maintenance mode only during migrations
  • Two Deploy Modes: Pull (on-server build) and ship (local build + rsync to remote)
  • Frappe Cloud Sync: Import apps, Python deps, and database backups from Frappe Cloud
  • Smart Rollback: Automatic rollback on migration failures, instant manual rollback to any previous release
  • Flexible Hooks: Customize every phase of build and deployment with shell scripts
  • CI/CD Ready: GitHub Actions integration with dedicated action

Breaking Changes:

None (initial release)

Migration Guide:

For new installations, follow the Quick Start Guide.

items.title: Contributing
items.text: Found a bug or have a feature request? Open an issue or discussion on GitHub.

items.title: FAQ
items.text: Frequently asked questions about fmd.

items.title: What is fmd?
items.text: fmd (Frappe Manager Deployer) is a zero-downtime deployment tool for Frappe applications. It provides atomic releases, instant rollback, and automated migrations.

items.title: How does fmd relate to Frappe Manager?
items.text:
fmd works alongside Frappe Manager (fm):

  • Frappe Manager: Creates and manages Frappe benches in Docker
  • fmd: Handles deployments with versioned releases on top of FM benches

You need both: FM for the runtime environment, fmd for deployment automation.

items.title: What's the difference between FM and fmd?
items.text: Feature Frappe Manager (fm) fmd Purpose Development environment Production deployment Scope Bench creation, Docker management Release management, migrations, rollback Target Developers DevOps, CI/CD pipelines Deployment Single bench state Versioned releases with history

items.title: Can I use fmd without Frappe Manager?
items.text: No. fmd requires a Frappe Manager bench as the foundation. It extends FM with production deployment capabilities.

items.title: Which Python version do I need?
items.text: Python 3.10 or later. fmd is tested on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, and 3.13.

items.title: Do I need to install Docker?
items.text: Yes. fmd uses Docker containers (via Frappe Manager) to build and run Frappe applications.

items.title: Can I use fmd on Windows?
items.text: fmd is designed for Linux and macOS. For Windows, use WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux).

items.title: How do I access private GitHub repositories?
items.text:
Set a GitHub personal access token:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Or add to your config file:

github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

items.title: What's the difference between pull and ship mode?
items.text:
• Pull: Build on production server, deploy locally (simpler, uses server resources)
  • Ship: Build locally/CI, rsync to remote (offloads builds, enables parallel deploys)

See Deploy Modes Guide for details.

items.title: How long does a deployment take?
items.text:
First deployment: 8-15 minutes (downloads dependencies, builds apps)

Subsequent deployments: 3-6 minutes (uses cache, only rebuilds changed apps)

Actual time depends on:

  • Number of apps
  • Server resources (CPU, RAM, network)
  • Cache hit rate

items.title: Can I deploy to multiple servers at once?
items.text:
Yes, with ship mode:

fmd deploy ship --config server1.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server2.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server3.toml

Build once, ship to multiple servers.

items.title: Will deployment cause downtime?
items.text:
Minimal (typically <1 second) during symlink switch.

Enable maintenance_mode = true for user-friendly maintenance page during migrations.

items.title: What happens if deployment fails?
items.text:
• If rollback = true: Automatically reverts to previous release
  • If rollback = false: Site stays on previous working release, new release is kept for debugging

Either way, your site stays up.

items.title: How many releases should I keep?
items.text:
Default is 7. This balances:

  • Disk space: Each release ~700 MB (Frappe + ERPNext)
  • Rollback options: 7 releases = ~1-2 weeks of history

Adjust with:

[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7

items.title: Can I rollback to any previous release?
items.text:
Yes, as long as the release still exists (not cleaned up):

fmd release list mysite.localhost
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

items.title: What happens to old releases?
items.text:
When you exceed releases_retain_limit, fmd automatically:

  1. Backs up old releases to deployment-backup/
  2. Deletes old releases from workspace

items.title: Can I manually delete releases?
items.text:
Yes:

fmd cleanup mysite.localhost -r 3 -y

Keeps 3 most recent releases, deletes the rest.

items.title: Can I sync from Frappe Cloud?
items.text:
Yes. fmd can import:

  • App list and commit hashes
  • Python version
  • Database backups

See Frappe Cloud Sync Guide.

items.title: Does fmd work with Frappe Cloud?
items.text:
fmd is for self-hosted deployments. It can sync from Frappe Cloud but doesn't deploy to Frappe Cloud.

Use case: Migrate from FC to self-hosted infrastructure.

items.title: Do I need Frappe Cloud credentials?
items.text: Only if you want to sync from FC. For standard deployments, no FC account needed.

items.title: Build fails with "GitHub API rate limit exceeded"
items.text:
Set a GitHub token:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

This increases rate limit from 60 to 5000 requests/hour.

items.title: Migration fails during deployment
items.text:
Check migration logs:

cat ~/frappe/sites/mysite/workspace/frappe-bench/.migrate_log

If rollback enabled, site automatically reverts to previous release.

items.title: "Permission denied" errors
items.text: Never use sudo with fmd or pip. Use uv tool or pipx instead (user-space installation).

items.title: Site not accessible after deployment
items.text:
Check bench status:

fm info mysite

If stopped:

fm start mysite

items.title: How do I enable debug logging?
items.text:
fmd -v deploy pull --config site.toml

The -v flag must come before the subcommand.

items.title: Can I customize the build process?
items.text:
Yes, use hooks. Example:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"
before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""

See Hooks Guide for details.

items.title: Can I use monorepo apps?
items.text:
Yes:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

See Monorepo Apps Guide.

items.title: Can I run workers on separate servers?
items.text:
Yes, use remote workers:

fmd remote-worker enable mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100

See Remote Workers Guide.

items.title: How do I change site domain?
items.text:
Use search-replace:

fmd search-replace mysite.localhost "old.com" "new.com" --dry-run
fmd search-replace mysite.localhost "old.com" "new.com"

Then update site config and nginx.

items.title: Can I deploy custom branches?
items.text:
Yes:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost --app frappe/frappe:my-feature-branch

Or in config:

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "my-feature-branch"

items.title: Where can I get support?
items.text:
• Documentation: https://rtcamp.github.io/fmd/
  • GitHub Issues: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/issues
  • Discussions: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/discussions

items.title: How do I report a bug?
items.text:
Open an issue: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/issues/new

Include:

  • fmd version (fmd --version)
  • Full command and output
  • Relevant logs
  • Config file (remove secrets)

items.title: How do I request a feature?
items.text: Start a discussion: https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/discussions/new

items.title: Is fmd production-ready?
items.text: fmd is actively developed and used in production by rtCamp. However, always test deployments in staging first.

items.title: Commands
items.text:
Complete reference for all fmd CLI commands.

Command help

Use fmd <command> --help to see detailed options and examples for any command.

Global options

  • fmd -v <command> — Enable verbose logging (must precede subcommand)
  • -c, --config <path> — Load TOML config file (accepted by all commands)

items.title: Quick Overview
items.text:
• Deploy

    Full automated deployment workflows: configure → create → switch

    fmd deploy pull --config site.toml
fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

  • Release Management

    Manual control over release lifecycle for advanced workflows

    fmd release configure mysite.localhost
fmd release create mysite.localhost
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

  • Remote Workers

    Configure and sync releases to remote worker servers

    fmd remote-worker enable mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100
fmd remote-worker sync mysite.localhost --rw-server 192.168.1.100

  • Cleanup

    Clean up old releases and backups to free disk space

    fmd cleanup mysite.localhost -r 3 -b 5 -y

items.title: All Commands
items.text: Detailed documentation for each command is auto-generated from the CLI. Click the links above or navigate using the sidebar.

items.title: fmd cleanup
items.text:
Cleanup deployment backups and releases.

Usage:

$ fmd cleanup

items.title: Cleanup and auto-approve
items.text:
Runs cleanup without prompting for confirmation. Useful in CI or scripts.

fmd cleanup --config {config_path} --yes

items.title: Keep last 3 releases
items.text:
Deletes all but the 3 most recent releases in the bench workspace.

fmd cleanup mysite --release-retain-limit 3

items.title: fmd deploy
items.text:
Deploy commands.

Usage:

$ fmd deploy [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:

  • --help: Show this message and exit.

Commands:

  • pull: Full deploy: configure (if needed) → create release → switch.
  • ship: Ship deploy: create release locally → rsync to remote → switch on remote.

items.title: fmd deploy pull
items.text:
Full deploy: configure (if needed) → create release → switch.

Usage:

$ fmd deploy pull

items.title: Deploy with Frappe Cloud integration
items.text:
Pulls FC app list and DB backup, then deploys. Requires FC API credentials.

fmd deploy pull {bench_name} --fc-key {fc_key} --fc-secret {fc_secret} --fc-site {fc_site}

items.title: Deploy with explicit apps
items.text:
Overrides config apps list with specified repos and refs.

fmd deploy pull mysite --app frappe/frappe:version-15 --app frappe/erpnext:version-15

items.title: Deploy from config file
items.text:
Reads bench name and app list from the TOML config file. Full deploy: configure → create → switch.

fmd deploy pull --config {config_path}

items.title: fmd deploy ship
items.text:
Ship deploy: create release locally → rsync to remote → switch on remote.

Usage:

$ fmd deploy ship

items.title: Skip rsync (release already on remote)
items.text:
Switches to an already-transferred release without rsyncing again. Useful for retrying a failed switch.

fmd deploy ship --config {config_path} --existing-release {release_name} --skip-rsync

items.title: Ship using an existing release
items.text:
Skips release creation and rsyncs an already-created local release to the remote, then switches.

fmd deploy ship --config {config_path} --existing-release {release_name}

items.title: Ship from config file
items.text:
Creates a release locally, rsyncs it to the remote server, and switches the live bench. Requires a [ship] section in config.

fmd deploy ship --config {config_path}

items.title: fmd release
items.text:
Release commands.

Usage:

$ fmd release [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:

  • --help: Show this message and exit.

Commands:

  • configure: One-time setup: converts a plain bench into a versioned release structure.
  • create: Create a new release: clone apps, build assets, no live bench changes.
  • switch: Switch live bench symlink to a previously-created release.
  • list: List all releases, marking the currently active one.
  • info: Show release info by inspecting each app's git repository.

items.title: fmd release configure
items.text:
One-time setup: converts a plain bench into a versioned release structure.

Usage:

$ fmd release configure

items.title: Configure from config file
items.text:
Reads bench name and apps from the TOML config and runs one-time release structure setup.

fmd release configure --config {config_path}

items.title: Configure from bench name
items.text:
Converts a plain bench into a versioned release structure using bench name to locate it.

fmd release configure mysite

items.title: fmd release create
items.text:
Create a new release: clone apps, build assets, no live bench changes.

Usage:

$ fmd release create

items.title: Create with explicit apps
items.text:
Overrides the apps list from config with the specified repos and refs.

fmd release create mysite --app frappe/frappe:version-15 --app frappe/erpnext:version-15

items.title: Create in image mode with build dir
items.text:
Builds the release as a standalone directory outside the bench. Activates image mode automatically.

fmd release create {bench_name} --build-dir {build_dir}

items.title: Create from config file
items.text:
Clones apps, builds assets, and writes a new release directory. No live bench changes.

fmd release create --config {config_path}

items.title: fmd release switch
items.text:
Switch live bench symlink to a previously-created release.

Usage:

$ fmd release switch

items.title: Switch with maintenance mode
items.text:
Enables maintenance mode during the switch to prevent user-facing errors while the symlink is updated.

fmd release switch {bench_name} {release_name} --maintenance-mode

items.title: Switch with migrate
items.text:
Runs bench migrate after switching the symlink to apply any pending schema changes.

fmd release switch {bench_name} {release_name} --migrate

items.title: Switch to a release
items.text:
Updates the live bench symlink to point at the specified release directory.

fmd release switch {bench_name} {release_name}

items.title: fmd release list
items.text:
List all releases, marking the currently active one.

Usage:

$ fmd release list

items.title: List from config file
items.text:
Reads bench name from config and lists all releases with metadata.

fmd release list --config {config_path}

items.title: List releases by bench name
items.text:
Lists all releases in the bench workspace, marking the currently active one.

fmd release list mysite

items.title: fmd release info
items.text:
Show release info by inspecting each app's git repository.

Usage:

$ fmd release info

items.title: Info from config file
items.text:
Reads bench path from config and shows git info for all apps.

fmd release info --config {config_path}

items.title: Info by bench name
items.text:
Inspects each app's git repository in the bench and prints commit, branch, and tag info.

fmd release info mysite

items.title: fmd remote-worker
items.text:
Remote-Worker commands.

Usage:

$ fmd remote-worker [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:

  • --help: Show this message and exit.

Commands:

  • enable: Enable remote worker: expose DB + Redis ports, create worker site configs.
  • sync: Sync workspace to remote worker server.

items.title: fmd remote-worker enable
items.text:
Enable remote worker: expose DB + Redis ports, create worker site configs.

Usage:

$ fmd remote-worker enable

items.title: Enable from config file
items.text:
Reads bench name and remote worker settings from config, exposes DB/Redis ports and writes worker site configs.

fmd remote-worker enable --config {config_path}

items.title: Enable with bench name
items.text:
Enables remote worker for the specified bench, pointing at the given remote server IP.

fmd remote-worker enable {bench_name} --rw-server {rw_server}

items.title: fmd remote-worker sync
items.text:
Sync workspace to remote worker server.

Usage:

$ fmd remote-worker sync

items.title: Sync from config file
items.text:
Reads bench name and remote worker settings from config and syncs the workspace to the remote server.

fmd remote-worker sync --config {config_path}

items.title: Sync with bench name
items.text:
Syncs the workspace for the specified bench to the remote worker server.

fmd remote-worker sync {bench_name} --rw-server {rw_server}

items.title: fmd search-replace
items.text:
Search and replace text across all text fields in the Frappe database.

Usage:

$ fmd search-replace

items.title: Dry-run search-replace
items.text:
Shows what would be changed without modifying the database.

fmd search-replace {bench_name} {search} {replace} --dry-run

items.title: Search and replace in DB
items.text:
Replaces all occurrences of the search text across all text fields in the Frappe database.

fmd search-replace {bench_name} {search} {replace}

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Everything you need to go from zero to deploying Frappe sites with fmd.

  • Requirements

    Check what you need before installing — Python 3.10+, Docker, Frappe Manager, and a few minutes.

  • Installation

    Install fmd with uv, pipx, or from source. Configure GitHub tokens for private repositories.

  • Quick Start

    Deploy your first Frappe site step by step. Learn the most common deployment workflows and commands.

items.title: Installation
items.text: Install fmd using your preferred Python package manager.

items.title: Using uv (Recommended)
items.text: uv is a fast Python package installer and resolver.

items.title: Install as a tool
items.text:
uv tool install frappe-deployer

This installs fmd globally and makes the fmd command available.

items.title: Run without installing
items.text:
uvx frappe-deployer --help

This runs fmd directly from PyPI without permanent installation.

items.title: Install development version
items.text: uv tool install git+https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd@main

items.title: Upgrade
items.text: uv tool upgrade frappe-deployer

items.title: Install
items.text: pipx install frappe-deployer

items.title: Install development version
items.text: pipx install git+https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd@main

items.title: Upgrade
items.text: pipx upgrade frappe-deployer

items.title: Clone the repository
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd.git
cd fmd

items.title: Install in development mode
items.text:
pip install -e .

This creates an editable installation — changes to the source code take effect immediately.

items.title: Install with uv
items.text: uv pip install -e .

items.title: Verify Installation
items.text:
Check that fmd is installed correctly:

fmd --version

You should see output like:

fmd version 0.1.0

Check available commands:

fmd --help

items.title: GitHub Token
items.text:
Set up your GitHub token for private repositories:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Add to your shell profile (~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, etc.) to persist:

echo 'export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here' >> ~/.bashrc

Alternatively, add it to your config file (see Configuration Guide).

items.title: Frappe Cloud (optional)
items.text:
If you plan to sync from Frappe Cloud, set up credentials:

export FC_API_KEY=your_api_key
export FC_API_SECRET=your_api_secret

Or add to your config file:

[fc]
api_key = "your_api_key"
api_secret = "your_api_secret"
site_name = "yoursite.frappe.cloud"
team_name = "your-team"

items.title: Command not found
items.text:
If fmd command is not found after installation:

uv/pipx: Ensure the tool installation directory is in your PATH:

echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

From source: Ensure you're in the correct virtual environment or the installation directory is in your PATH.

items.title: Permission denied
items.text:
If you get permission errors during installation:

Never use sudo with pip

Don't use sudo pip install. This breaks system Python and causes permission issues. Always use uv tool, pipx, or user-space installations.

Recommended: Use uv tool or pipx (they install in user space automatically).

If you must use pip directly:

pip install --user frappe-deployer

items.title: Network issues behind proxy
items.text:
Configure proxy settings:

export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
uv tool install frappe-deployer

items.title: Next Steps
items.text: Now that fmd is installed, proceed to the Quick Start guide to deploy your first site.

items.title: Quick Start
items.text: This guide walks you through your first deployment with fmd — from zero to a running Frappe site.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Make sure you have:

  • Python 3.10+ installed
  • Docker and Docker Compose V2 running
  • Frappe Manager installed (fm --version works)
  • fmd installed (fmd --version works)

If not, see Requirements and Installation.

items.title: Step 1: Create a Frappe Manager Bench
items.text:
First, create a bench using Frappe Manager. This sets up the Docker environment where your site will run.

fm create mysite --environment prod

This creates a production bench named mysite. The bench directory is created at:

~/frappe/sites/mysite/

Development vs Production

Use --environment dev for development benches with developer tools enabled.

items.title: Step 2: Configure Your Deployment Workspace
items.text:
Navigate to your bench directory and configure the fmd workspace:

cd ~/frappe/sites/mysite
fmd release configure mysite.localhost

This creates the workspace structure:

~/frappe/sites/mysite/workspace/
├── .cache/              # Build cache
└── deployment-data/     # Persistent data (sites, logs, config)

items.title: Step 3: Deploy Frappe + ERPNext
items.text:
Configuration Files for Repeated Deployments

Before running your first deployment, consider creating a site.toml file (see Step 6 below). This makes future deployments cleaner and easier to track in version control.

Now deploy your apps with a single command:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode \
  --backups

What happens:

  1. Creates a new timestamped release (e.g., release_20260416_143022)
  2. Clones app repositories from GitHub
  3. Builds Frappe bench inside Docker container
  4. Enables maintenance mode
  5. Takes database backup
  6. Runs migrations
  7. Switches to the new release atomically (symlink update)
  8. Restarts services
  9. Disables maintenance mode

This typically takes 5-15 minutes depending on your internet speed and server resources.

items.title: Step 4: Verify Deployment
items.text:
Check that your site is running:

fmd info mysite.localhost

You should see output showing:

  • Current release (e.g., release_20260416_143022)
  • Installed apps (frappe, erpnext)
  • Site status

List all releases:

fmd release list mysite.localhost

items.title: Step 5: Access Your Site
items.text:
Default Password Security

Change the default admin password immediately after first login. Go to User Settings → Change Password in the Frappe UI.

Your site is now live! Access it at:

http://mysite.localhost

Default credentials:

  • Username: Administrator
  • Password: admin (or the password you set during bench creation)

items.title: Using a Configuration File
items.text:
Best Practice: Use Configuration Files

Command-line flags are great for quick tests, but configuration files provide version control, reproducibility, and team collaboration benefits. Create a site.toml file and commit it to your repository.

For repeated deployments, use a config file instead of command-line flags.

Create site.toml:

site_name = "mysite.localhost"
bench_name = "mysite"
github_token = "ghp_your_token"  # Optional, for private repos

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
backups = true

Then deploy with:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

Much cleaner! See Configuration Guide for all available options.

items.title: Update Apps (New Deployment)
items.text:
To deploy updates (e.g., new app versions or code changes):

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

This creates a new release with the latest code and switches to it.

items.title: Rollback to Previous Release
items.text:
If something goes wrong, instantly rollback:

fmd release list mysite.localhost
# Find the previous release timestamp
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_20260415_120000

items.title: Deploy with Custom Python Version
items.text:
fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --python-version 3.11

items.title: Deploy a Monorepo App
items.text:
For apps in subdirectories of a monorepo:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app my-org/monorepo:main:apps/my-app

Format: org/repo:ref:subdir_path

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
Now that you've deployed your first site, explore:

  • Deploy Modes — Learn about pull vs ship deployment strategies
  • GitHub Actions — Automate deployments from CI/CD
  • Hooks — Customize build and deployment lifecycle
  • Frappe Cloud Sync — Sync apps and databases from Frappe Cloud
  • Configuration — Deep dive into all config options

items.title: Build fails during deployment
items.text:
Check logs:

fm logs mysite

Common issues:

  • GitHub rate limit: Set GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable
  • Network timeout: Increase timeout or check internet connection
  • Disk space: Ensure at least 5 GB free space

items.title: Site not accessible
items.text:
Verify the bench is running:

fm info mysite

If stopped, start it:

fm start mysite

items.title: Migration errors
items.text:
If migrations fail, fmd auto-rollbacks to the previous release (if rollback = true in config).

Check which release is active:

fmd info mysite.localhost

Manually switch if needed:

fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

For more troubleshooting, see Troubleshooting Guide.

items.title: Requirements
items.text: Before installing fmd, make sure you have the following:

items.title: Python 3.10+
items.text:
fmd requires Python 3.10 or later.

Check your Python version:

python3 --version

If you need to install or upgrade Python:

macOS (Homebrew)Ubuntu/DebianRHEL/CentOS/Rocky 
brew install python@3.11

sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.11 python3.11-venv python3-pip

sudo dnf install python3.11

items.title: Docker
items.text:
Docker is required for building and running Frappe containers.

Check if Docker is installed:

docker --version
docker compose version

If you need to install Docker:

  • Linux: Follow the official Docker installation guide
  • macOS: Install Docker Desktop for Mac
  • Windows: Install Docker Desktop for Windows

Docker Compose V2

fmd requires Docker Compose V2 (the docker compose command, not the legacy docker-compose).

items.title: Frappe Manager
items.text:
fmd works alongside Frappe Manager to manage Frappe benches.

Install Frappe Manager:

uv tool install --python 3.13 frappe-manager

Or with pipx:

pipx install frappe-manager

Verify installation:

fm --version

items.title: GitHub Personal Access Token
items.text:
Required for:

  • Deploying private repositories
  • Avoiding GitHub API rate limits

Create a token at github.com/settings/tokens with repo scope.

Store it securely:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

Or add to your config file:

github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

items.title: SSH Access (for remote deployments)
items.text:
If you plan to use fmd deploy ship or remote workers, you'll need:

  • SSH access to your production server
  • Passwordless SSH key authentication configured

Test SSH access:

ssh user@your-server.com

items.title: Frappe Cloud Credentials (optional)
items.text:
If you want to sync from Frappe Cloud, you'll need:

  • Frappe Cloud API key and secret
  • Team name and site name

Get these from your Frappe Cloud dashboard.

items.title: Disk Space
items.text:
Each release requires disk space for:

  • Apps repository clones
  • Python virtual environment
  • Node modules
  • Built assets

Minimum recommended: 5 GB free space per site workspace

Actual usage depends on:

  • Number of apps installed
  • Number of releases retained (default: 7)
  • Backup retention policy

items.title: Network Requirements
items.text:
fmd needs network access to:

  • GitHub: Clone app repositories
  • PyPI: Install Python packages
  • npm registry: Install Node packages
  • Docker Hub/GHCR: Pull container images

If deploying behind a proxy, configure:

export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080

items.title: Next Steps
items.text: Once you have all requirements installed, proceed to Installation.

items.title: Guides
items.text:
In-depth walkthroughs for everything fmd can do.

  • Deploy Modes

    Understand pull vs ship deployment strategies. Learn when to build on-server vs build locally and rsync.

  • Configuration

    Complete guide to the TOML configuration file. All options, defaults, and examples explained.

  • :lucide-github:{ .lg .middle }   GitHub Actions

    Automate deployments from CI/CD with the rtcamp/frappe-deployer GitHub Action. Pull and ship strategies.

  • Hooks & Lifecycle

    Customize every phase of the build and deployment process with shell hooks. Build assets, run tests, send notifications.

  • Frappe Cloud Sync

    Import app lists, Python dependencies, and database backups directly from Frappe Cloud. Perfect for migrations.

  • Maintenance Mode

    Control when maintenance mode activates. Bypass tokens for developer access during deployments.

  • Remote Workers

    Run background workers on separate servers. Sync releases across machines for distributed setups.

  • Monorepo Apps

    Deploy apps from subdirectories of monorepos. Symlink for efficient workspace management.

  • Rollback & Recovery

    Instant rollback to previous releases. Automatic rollback on migration failures. Backup and restore workflows.

items.title: Configuration
items.text: Complete guide for the fmd configuration file (site.toml).

items.title: Quick Start
items.text: fmd uses TOML configuration files to define deployment settings. This eliminates long command-line flags and makes configurations reusable and version-controllable.

items.title: Minimal Configuration
items.text:
For a basic production deployment with Frappe + ERPNext:

site_name = "mysite.localhost"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
maintenance_mode = true
backups = true
migrate = true

Deploy with:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

That's it! This configuration:

  • ✅ Deploys Frappe v15 + ERPNext v15
  • ✅ Enables maintenance mode during migration
  • ✅ Takes automatic backups before switching
  • ✅ Runs database migrations

items.title: Configuration File Location
items.text:
Store your config file anywhere:

  • Project root: site.toml
  • CI/CD: .github/configs/site.toml
  • Per-environment: config/staging.toml, config/prod.toml

Reference it with --config:

fmd deploy pull --config .github/configs/site.toml

items.title: Private Repositories
items.text:
GitHub Token Best Practices

Never commit tokens directly to configuration files. Use environment variable substitution (github_token = "${GITHUB_TOKEN}") and store tokens in CI/CD secrets or local environment variables. Generate tokens at github.com/settings/tokens with repo scope.

github_token = "${GITHUB_TOKEN}"  # Environment variable substitution

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/private-app"
ref = "main"

Set the token via environment:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxx
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

items.title: Monorepo Apps
items.text:
For apps in subdirectories within a repository:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

See Monorepo Apps Guide for more details.

items.title: Custom Build Hooks
items.text:
Run custom scripts during deployment lifecycle:

[release.hooks]
before_install_all = "echo 'Starting installation...'"
after_build_apps = """
bench --site $SITE_NAME set-config developer_mode 0
bench --site $SITE_NAME clear-cache
"""

[switch.hooks]
before_restart = "supervisorctl stop all"
after_restart = "curl -f http://localhost:8000 || exit 1"

See Hooks Guide for all available hook points.

items.title: Ship Mode (Build Locally, Deploy Remotely)
items.text:
For offloading builds to CI runners or deploying to multiple servers:

site_name = "mysite.example.com"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[ship]
host = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

[switch]
migrate = true
backups = true

Deploy with:

fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

See Deploy Modes Guide for pull vs ship comparison.

items.title: Environment Variable Substitution
items.text:
Configuration files support environment variable substitution using ${VAR_NAME} or $VAR_NAME syntax. This allows dynamic configuration based on environment:

site_name = "${SITE_NAME}"
github_token = "${GITHUB_TOKEN}"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "${FRAPPE_VERSION}"

[[apps]]
repo = "${GITHUB_ORG}/custom-app"
ref = "main"

[switch]
backups = "${BACKUPS_ENABLED}"

Supported patterns: - ${VAR_NAME} - Braced syntax (recommended) - $VAR_NAME - Unbraced syntax - Variable names must start with letter or underscore, contain only uppercase letters, numbers, and underscores

Behavior: - Defined variables are replaced with their values - Undefined variables preserve original syntax: ${UNDEFINED} remains as-is - Works in any string value (nested objects, lists, mixed strings) - Non-string values (numbers, booleans) are not affected

Usage with GitHub Actions:

- name: Deploy
  uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@fmx/0
  env:
    SITE_NAME: helpdesk.example.com
    FRAPPE_VERSION: version-15
    GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    BACKUPS_ENABLED: "true"
  with:
    config_file: .github/deploy.toml
Full Configuration Reference

Complete documentation of all available configuration options.

items.title: Site and Bench
items.text:
site_name = "mysite.localhost"

The Frappe site name. This must match the site created in Frappe Manager.

bench_name = ""

The bench/container identifier. Defaults to site_name if not specified. Only set this if your bench directory name differs from your site name.

items.title: Apps Configuration
items.text: Define apps to install with [[apps]] sections. Each app is a separate block.

items.title: Basic App
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"
  • repo: Repository in format "org/repo" or full GitHub URL
  • ref: Git branch, tag, or commit hash

items.title: Multiple Apps
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/hrms"
ref = "main"

Apps are installed in the order specified.

items.title: Private Repositories
items.text:
github_token = "ghp_your_token_here"

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/private-app"
ref = "main"

Set github_token at the top level to access private repositories.

items.title: Monorepo Apps
items.text:
For apps in subdirectories:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

See Monorepo Apps Guide for details.

items.title: Symlinked Apps (Development)
items.text:
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "develop"
symlink = true

Symlink instead of copying — useful for development where you want to edit code directly.

### Clone Options

```toml
[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"
shallow_clone = true      # Default: true
remote_name = "upstream"  # Default: "upstream"
```

- `shallow_clone`: Use `--depth=1` for faster cloning (saves bandwidth and disk)
- `remote_name`: Git remote name

## Release Configuration

Control how releases are created and managed.

```toml
[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7
python_version = "3.11"
node_version = "20"
```

### Retention Limit

```toml
releases_retain_limit = 7
```

Number of releases to keep. Older releases are automatically deleted. Default: 7

### Python and Node Versions

```toml
python_version = "3.11"
node_version = "20"
```

Pin specific versions for your release. If not specified, uses system defaults.

### Runner Mode

```toml
mode = "exec"  # or "image"
```

- `exec` (default): Run build in existing docker-compose containers
- `image`: Run build in temporary containers (works without running services)

### Platform (Cross-Architecture)

```toml
platform = "linux/arm64"
```

Force a specific Docker platform. Useful for cross-architecture deployments (e.g., build on x86_64, deploy to ARM64).

## Switch Configuration

Control behavior during release switch (activation).

```toml
[switch]
migrate = true
migrate_timeout = 300
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false
search_replace = true
```

### Migration

```toml
migrate = true
migrate_timeout = 300
```

- `migrate`: Run `bench migrate` during switch
- `migrate_timeout`: Timeout in seconds for migrations (default: 300)

### Maintenance Mode

```toml
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
```

- `maintenance_mode`: Enable maintenance mode during switch
- `maintenance_mode_phases`: When to enable (options: `"drain"`, `"migrate"`)

See [Maintenance Mode Guide](maintenance-mode.md) for details.

### Backups

```toml
backups = true
backup_timeout = 600
```

- `backups`: Take database backup before switch
- `backup_timeout`: Timeout in seconds (default: 600)

### Rollback

```toml
rollback = false
```

Automatically rollback to previous release if switch fails. Default: false

!!! warning "Auto-rollback Behavior"
    When `rollback = false` and a deployment fails, you'll need to manually investigate and fix the issue or rollback via `fmd release switch`. When `rollback = true`, fmd silently reverts to the previous release — which can hide problems. Only enable auto-rollback in automated environments where immediate investigation isn't possible.

### Search and Replace

```toml
search_replace = true
search_replace_pairs = []
```

Run search-replace operations during switch. See [Search-Replace Command](../commands/search-replace.md).

### Worker Draining

```toml
drain_workers = false
drain_workers_timeout = 300
skip_stale_workers = true
skip_stale_timeout = 15
worker_kill_timeout = 15
```

Control background worker behavior during switch:

- `drain_workers`: Wait for workers to finish jobs before switch
- `drain_workers_timeout`: Max time to wait for workers (seconds)
- `skip_stale_workers`: Skip workers that haven't processed jobs recently
- `skip_stale_timeout`: Consider worker stale after N seconds of inactivity
- `worker_kill_timeout`: Force-kill workers after this timeout

## Frappe Cloud Integration

Sync apps, dependencies, or database from Frappe Cloud.

```toml
[fc]
api_key = "fc_your_key"
api_secret = "fc_your_secret"
site_name = "yoursite.frappe.cloud"
team_name = "your-team"

[release]
use_fc_apps = true
use_fc_deps = true

[switch]
use_fc_db = true
```

- `use_fc_apps`: Import app list and commit hashes from FC
- `use_fc_deps`: Import Python version from FC
- `use_fc_db`: Download and restore latest FC database backup

See [Frappe Cloud Sync Guide](frappe-cloud.md) for details.

## Ship Mode (Remote Deployment)

Configure remote server for ship mode deployments.

```toml
[ship]
server_ip = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22
remote_workspace_root = "/home/frappe/frappe/sites/mysite.localhost/workspace"
```

See [Deploy Modes Guide](deploy-modes.md#ship-mode) for details.

## Remote Workers

Enable remote workers to run background jobs on separate servers.

```toml
[remote_worker]
server_ip = "192.168.1.200"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22
```

See [Remote Workers Guide](remote-workers.md) for details.

## Hooks

Customize build and deployment lifecycle with shell hooks.

### App-Level Hooks

```toml
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"

before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""

after_bench_build = """
echo "Build complete for my-app"
"""
```

### Global Hooks

```toml
[release]
before_bench_build = """
echo "Starting build..."
"""

after_bench_build = """
echo "Build complete!"
"""
```

### Host Hooks

Prefix with `host_` to run on host instead of inside container:

```toml
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"

host_after_bench_build = """
curl -X POST "$WEBHOOK_URL" -d "Build complete"
"""
```

### Available Hooks

**Build phase (per-app and global):**

- `before_bench_build`
- `after_bench_build`
- `before_python_install`
- `after_python_install`

**Switch phase (global only):**

- `before_restart`
- `after_restart`
- `host_before_restart`
- `host_after_restart`

See [Hooks Guide](hooks.md) for detailed examples.

## Environment Variables in Hooks

Hooks have access to these environment variables:

- `$SITE_NAME`: Site name
- `$APP_NAME`: Current app name (app-level hooks only)
- `$RELEASE_DIR`: Release directory path
- `$WORKSPACE_ROOT`: Workspace root path

Custom env vars via `app_env`:

```toml
[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/my-app"
ref = "main"
app_env = { API_KEY = "secret123", ENV = "production" }
```

## Command-Line Overrides

Most config file options can be overridden via CLI flags:

```bash
fmd deploy pull --config site.toml \
  --maintenance-mode \
  --python-version 3.12 \
  --app frappe/frappe:develop
```

CLI flags take precedence over config file values.

## Multiple Environments

Use separate config files for different environments:

```
.github/configs/
├── dev.toml
├── staging.toml
└── prod.toml
```

Then deploy with:

```bash
fmd deploy pull --config .github/configs/prod.toml
```

## Validation

fmd validates your config before deployment:

- Required fields present
- Valid TOML syntax
- Reasonable timeout values
- App format correctness

Validation errors are shown immediately — fmd won't start building with invalid config.

## Next Steps

- See [example-config.toml](https://github.com/rtcamp/fmd/blob/main/example-config.toml) for complete schema
- Learn about [hooks](hooks.md) for customization
- Set up [GitHub Actions](github-actions.md) for CI/CD

items.title: Deploy Modes
items.text: fmd supports two deployment strategies: pull and ship. Each has different use cases and tradeoffs.

items.title: Overview
items.text: Mode Build Location Deploy Target When to Use pull Production server Same server Standard deployments, server has build capacity ship CI runner/local Remote via SSH Offload builds, cross-arch, multi-server deploy

items.title: Pull Mode
items.text:
Build on the production server, deploy on the same server.

When to Choose Pull Mode

Pull mode is ideal when your production server has adequate CPU/RAM for builds (4+ cores, 8+ GB RAM) and you value simplicity over build isolation. For resource-constrained servers or multi-server deployments, consider ship mode instead.

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
1. Connect to production server where Frappe Manager bench exists
  2. Run fmd deploy pull directly on the server
  3. fmd creates a new release by building inside Docker on that server
  4. Switch to new release atomically

items.title: Usage
items.text:
fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app frappe/frappe:version-15 \
  --app frappe/erpnext:version-15 \
  --maintenance-mode --backups

Or with a config file:

fmd deploy pull --config site.toml

items.title: Advantages
items.text:
• Simple setup: No SSH configuration needed
  • Direct control: Run commands directly on production
  • Immediate feedback: See build logs in real-time
  • No file transfer: Everything happens locally

items.title: Disadvantages
items.text:
• Server resource usage: Build consumes CPU/RAM on production
  • Potential downtime: Heavy builds can affect running services
  • Sequential only: Can't deploy to multiple servers in parallel
  • Architecture-specific: Must build on same arch as production

items.title: When to Use Pull
items.text:
• Small to medium deployments
  • Server has enough CPU/RAM to build without affecting services
  • Single-server setup
  • You have direct SSH access to production

items.title: Example: Pull from CI
items.text:
Even in CI, you can SSH to production and run pull:

- name: Deploy via pull
  run: |
    ssh user@prod "cd /path/to/bench && \
      fmd deploy pull --config site.toml"

items.title: Ship Mode
items.text:
Build locally or in CI, then ship to remote production server.

When to Choose Ship Mode

Ship mode excels in three scenarios: (1) deploying to multiple servers from a single build, (2) offloading builds from resource-constrained production servers, and (3) cross-architecture deployments (e.g., build on x86, deploy to ARM). The tradeoff is more complex setup with SSH keys and rsync configuration.

items.title: How It Works
items.text: Ship mode coordinates a multi-step deployment workflow:

items.title: 1. Local Build Phase
items.text:
• Runs fmd release create in Docker on CI runner/local machine
  • Pulls runner image (e.g., ghcr.io/rtcamp/fmd-runner:latest)
  • Installs Python dependencies via uv
  • Installs Node.js dependencies
  • Builds production assets (JS, CSS)
  • Creates immutable release directory: workspace/release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS/

items.title: 2. Transfer Phase
items.text:
• Rsyncs entire release directory to remote server
  • Transfers TOML config file
  • Ensures remote has uv/uvx installed for running fmd

items.title: 3. Remote Configuration Phase
items.text:
• Runs fmd release configure on remote via uvx
  • Creates bench directory structure if not exists
  • Symlinks apps into bench
  • No git access required (uses pre-built artifacts)

items.title: 4. Remote Switch Phase
items.text:
• Runs fmd release switch to activate new release
  • Updates workspace/frappe-bench symlink to new release
  • Optionally drains workers, enables maintenance mode
  • Runs migrations if configured
  • Takes backups if configured
  • Restarts services

Key insight: Ship builds locally but delegates configuration/switch to remote fmd instance via uvx.

items.title: Usage
items.text:
Requires a [ship] section in your config:

[ship]
server_ip = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22
remote_workspace_root = "/home/frappe/frappe/sites/mysite.localhost/workspace"

Then deploy:

fmd deploy ship --config site.toml

items.title: Advantages
items.text:
• Offload builds: Free up production resources
  • Parallel deploys: Ship same release to multiple servers
  • Cross-architecture: Build on x86, deploy to ARM (or vice versa)
  • Faster deploys: Rsync is faster than rebuilding on each server
  • Pre-validated: Test release locally before shipping

items.title: Disadvantages
items.text:
• Complex setup: Requires SSH keys, rsync, proper permissions
  • Network dependency: Large file transfer over network
  • Initial slowness: First build downloads all dependencies
  • Storage overhead: Need disk space on both machines

items.title: When to Use Ship
items.text:
• Multiple production servers (deploy once, ship to many)
  • Limited production resources (small VPS, shared hosting)
  • Cross-architecture deployments
  • Want to test release locally before production
  • CI/CD pipeline with powerful runners

items.title: Example: Ship from GitHub Actions
items.text:
- name: Deploy via ship
  uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
  with:
    command: ship
    config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
    gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
    ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}

See GitHub Actions Guide for complete setup.

items.title: Hybrid Approach
items.text:
You can mix modes:

  • Development: Use pull mode on staging server
  • Production: Use ship mode for controlled releases

Or:

  • Regular deploys: Use pull for convenience
  • Emergency deploys: Use ship to build on powerful local machine

items.title: Build Time (Frappe + ERPNext, 4-core server)
items.text: Mode First Build Subsequent Notes pull ~8-12 min ~3-5 min Uses .cache on server ship ~10-15 min ~4-6 min Initial download, then rsync delta

items.title: Network Transfer (ship mode)
items.text:
Release Content Size Transfer Time (1 Gbps) Apps + venv ~500 MB ~4-5 seconds Node modules ~200 MB ~2 seconds Built assets ~50 MB <1 second Total ~750 MB ~7-10 seconds

Subsequent deploys transfer only changed files (much smaller).

items.title: Choosing a Mode
items.text:
Use this decision tree:

Do you have multiple production servers?
├─ YES → Use ship (build once, deploy to all)
└─ NO → Does production server have >4 CPU cores and >8 GB RAM?
    ├─ YES → Use pull (simpler)
    └─ NO → Use ship (offload builds)

items.title: Advanced: Custom Build Server
items.text:
You can designate a dedicated build server:

  1. Build server: Runs fmd in ship mode, builds releases
  2. Production servers: Receive releases via rsync, only run switch

This separates concerns:

  • Build server handles dependency downloads, compilations
  • Production servers only run application code

Example workflow:

# On build server
fmd deploy ship --config server1.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server2.toml
fmd deploy ship --config server3.toml

All three servers receive the same pre-built release.

items.text:

  • Configure hooks to customize build process
  • Understand release lifecycle

items.title: GitHub Action
items.text: Automate Frappe deployments from GitHub Actions using two strategies: pull (remote server pulls and builds) and ship (CI runner builds, rsyncs to remote).

items.title: Strategies
items.text: Strategy Build location Deploy target When to use pull Remote server Same server Server has enough CPU/RAM to build; simpler setup ship CI runner (Docker) Remote server via rsync Offload build from server; cross-arch builds

items.title: pull
items.text:
The CI runner SSHes into the remote server and runs fmd deploy pull there. The server clones apps, builds the release, and switches — all on the server itself.

Required secrets/vars:

Name Type Description GH_TOKEN secret GitHub token for private repo access SSH_PRIVATE_KEY secret Private key for the remote server SSH_SERVER secret Remote server hostname or IP SSH_USER secret SSH username (e.g. frappe) SITE_NAME var Frappe site name (e.g. mysite.example.com) SSH_PORT var SSH port — optional, defaults to 22

Minimal workflow (.github/workflows/deploy-pull.yml):

name: Deploy (pull)

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy via pull
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull
          sitename: ${{ vars.SITE_NAME }}
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}

With all switch options:

      - name: Deploy via pull
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull
          sitename: ${{ vars.SITE_NAME }}
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
          ssh_port: ${{ vars.SSH_PORT || '22' }}
          migrate: "true"
          migrate_timeout: "300"
          drain_workers: "true"
          drain_workers_timeout: "300"
          skip_stale_workers: "true"
          maintenance_mode_phases: "migrate"
          worker_kill_timeout: "15"

items.title: ship
items.text:
The CI runner builds the release locally inside Docker (no Frappe Manager services needed), rsyncs it to the remote server, then SSHes in to run fmd release switch.

The config file must include a [ship] section pointing at the remote server:

[ship]
host = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

Required secrets/vars: same as pull (GH_TOKEN, SSH_PRIVATE_KEY, SSH_SERVER, SSH_USER). No SITE_NAME — the site name comes from site_name in the TOML config.

Minimal workflow (.github/workflows/deploy-ship.yml):

name: Deploy (ship)

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Deploy via ship
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: ship
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}

With all switch options:

      - name: Deploy via ship
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: ship
          config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
          gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
          ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          ssh_server: ${{ secrets.SSH_SERVER }}
          ssh_user: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
          ssh_port: ${{ vars.SSH_PORT || '22' }}
          migrate: "true"
          migrate_timeout: "300"
          drain_workers: "true"
          drain_workers_timeout: "300"
          skip_stale_workers: "true"
          maintenance_mode_phases: "migrate"
          worker_kill_timeout: "15"
          runner_image: "ghcr.io/my-org/fm-builder:latest"

ship-only inputs:

Input Default Description runner_image auto-detected Docker image used to build the release. Override when you need a pinned or custom builder. existing_release — Skip the build step and deploy a release already present locally (format: release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS). skip_rsync false Skip the rsync step — useful when the release is already on the remote server.

items.title: Shared inputs
items.text:
Both pull and ship support the same switch-phase inputs:

Input Default Description migrate true Run bench migrate after switch migrate_timeout 300 Seconds before migrate is killed migrate_command — Override the migrate command entirely drain_workers false Gracefully drain background workers before restart drain_workers_timeout 300 Seconds to wait for workers to finish drain_workers_poll 5 Poll interval while draining (seconds) skip_stale_workers true Skip workers that haven't checked in recently skip_stale_timeout 15 Seconds before a worker is considered stale worker_kill_timeout 15 Seconds before force-killing workers worker_kill_poll 3.0 Poll interval while waiting to kill workers maintenance_mode_phases "" Space-separated phases to enable maintenance mode: drain, migrate additional_commands — Extra CLI flags appended to the fmd deploy invocation ssh_port 22 SSH port for the remote server config_path — Path to TOML config file, relative to repo root config_content — Inline TOML config string (alternative to config_path) app_env — Per-app env vars injected into build hooks — see Per-app environment

items.title: Per-app environment
items.text:
Inject secrets into app hook scripts without storing them in the config file:

      - name: Deploy
        uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@main
        with:
          command: pull   # or ship
          # ...
          app_env: |
            my-private-app:LICENSE_KEY=${{ secrets.MY_APP_LICENSE_KEY }}
            another-app:API_SECRET=${{ secrets.ANOTHER_API_SECRET }}

Format: one app-name:KEY=VALUE per line. The app-name prefix must match the Python module name of the app (last path segment of the repo, e.g. godam_core for rtcamp/godam-core). All declared vars are exported before any app's hook scripts run.

items.title: Hooks
items.text:
Each app can define up to 8 hooks in [[apps]]. The [switch] section has 4 more for restart-time.

Build-phase hooks (per-app, in [[apps]]):

Hook Runs in When before_bench_build container Before bench build for this app after_bench_build container After bench build for this app host_before_bench_build host Before bench build for this app host_after_bench_build host After bench build for this app before_python_install container Before uv pip install for this app after_python_install container After uv pip install for this app host_before_python_install host Before uv pip install for this app host_after_python_install host After uv pip install for this app

Switch-phase hooks (global, in [switch]):

Hook Runs in When before_restart container Before services restart after_restart container After services restart host_before_restart host Before services restart host_after_restart host After services restart

Hooks prefixed host_ run on the host machine. All others run inside the Docker container. Each hook value is either an inline shell script or a path to a .sh/.py file.

Example config with hooks:

[[apps]]
repo = "rtcamp/godam-core"
ref = "main"
before_bench_build = """
npm ci
npm run build:prod
"""
host_after_bench_build = """
curl -s -X POST "$WEBHOOK_URL" -d "app=$APP_NAME built"
"""

[switch]
host_before_restart = "echo 'switching to new release'"
after_restart = "bench --site $SITE_NAME clear-cache"

Hook Script Paths and Execution Context

Hook values are inline shell by default. If you reference a file path (ending in .sh/.py or starting with /, ./, ~/), fmd reads and executes that file — but it must exist on the machine running the hook at deploy time:

  • pull strategy: Scripts must exist on the remote server (not CI runner)
  • ship strategy: host_* hooks run on CI runner; other hooks run on remote server

Repo-committed scripts don't automatically transfer to the remote server. For portability, use inline scripts or ensure scripts are deployed separately.

Hook scripts receive these environment variables:

Variable Value BENCH_PATH Path to the frappe-bench symlink WORKSPACE_ROOT Path to the workspace root APPS Comma-separated list of installed app names SITE_NAME Frappe site name APP_NAME Current app name (build-phase hooks only) APP_PATH Path to the current app directory (build-phase hooks only)

Plus every field from the config as an uppercase env var (e.g. SITE_NAME, MIGRATE, PYTHON_VERSION).

For multiline values (e.g. a .env file), base64-encode the secret and pass via app_env:

          app_env: |
            my-app:DOTENV_B64=${{ secrets.MY_APP_DOTENV_B64 }}

Then decode inside the hook:

echo "${DOTENV_B64}" | base64 -d > /path/to/.env

items.title: Config file setup
items.text:
Both strategies load deployment configuration from a TOML file committed to the repo. Create .github/configs/site.toml:

For pull:

site_name = "mysite.example.com"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false

For ship (adds a [ship] section):

site_name = "mysite.example.com"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "version-15"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/erpnext"
ref = "version-15"

[ship]
host = "192.168.1.100"
ssh_user = "frappe"
ssh_port = 22

[switch]
migrate = true
maintenance_mode = true
maintenance_mode_phases = ["migrate"]
backups = true
rollback = false

With environment variable substitution:

Config files support ${VAR_NAME} or $VAR_NAME syntax for dynamic values from GitHub Actions environment:

site_name = "${SITE_NAME}"

[[apps]]
repo = "frappe/frappe"
ref = "${FRAPPE_VERSION}"

[[apps]]
repo = "${GITHUB_ORG}/custom-app"
ref = "main"

[ship]
host = "${SSH_SERVER}"
ssh_user = "${SSH_USER}"
ssh_port = 22

[switch]
migrate = true
backups = "${BACKUPS_ENABLED}"

Then in your workflow:

- name: Deploy
  uses: rtcamp/frappe-deployer@fmx/0
  env:
    SITE_NAME: mysite.example.com
    FRAPPE_VERSION: version-15
    GITHUB_ORG: my-org
    SSH_SERVER: 192.168.1.100
    SSH_USER: frappe
    BACKUPS_ENABLED: "true"
  with:
    command: ship
    config_path: .github/configs/site.toml
    gh_token: ${{ secrets.GH_TOKEN }}
    ssh_private_key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}

Undefined variables preserve original syntax (${UNDEFINED} stays as-is). See Configuration Guide for details.

See example-config.toml for the full schema.

items.title: Setting up secrets
items.text:
In your GitHub repository: Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions

Name Where Value GH_TOKEN Secrets GitHub PAT with repo scope (for private repos) or GITHUB_TOKEN for public SSH_PRIVATE_KEY Secrets Contents of the private key file (e.g. cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519) SSH_SERVER Secrets Remote server IP or hostname SSH_USER Secrets SSH username on the remote server SITE_NAME Variables Frappe site name — pull only SSH_PORT Variables SSH port if not 22 — optional

Generate a dedicated deploy key:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "github-actions-deploy" -f ~/.ssh/deploy_key -N ""
cat ~/.ssh/deploy_key        # → paste as SSH_PRIVATE_KEY secret
cat ~/.ssh/deploy_key.pub    # → append to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on remote server

items.title: Quick preview
items.text:
Deploy an app from a monorepo subdirectory:

fmd deploy pull mysite.localhost \
  --app my-org/monorepo:main:apps/my-app

Format: org/repo:ref:subdir_path

Or in configuration:

[[apps]]
repo = "my-org/monorepo"
ref = "main"
subdir_path = "apps/my-app"

items.title: How it works
items.text:
(Documentation in progress)

When you specify a subdir_path:

  1. fmd clones the full repository
  2. Creates a symlink from workspace to apps/my-app subdirectory
  3. Frappe bench sees it as a normal app
  4. Updates pull only changed files (efficient)

items.title: Architecture overview
items.text:
(Documentation in progress)

┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐
│  Web Server │         │Worker Server│
│  (Primary)  │────────▶│  (Remote)   │
│             │  rsync  │             │
│ fmd release │         │ fmd release │
└─────────────┘         └─────────────┘
  • Web server runs web processes and Frappe bench
  • Worker server runs background workers only
  • Releases sync automatically during deployment
  • Workers drain before release switch

items.text:
Rollback to a previous release:

# List available releases
fmd release list mysite.localhost

# Rollback to specific release
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_20260420_100000

Automatic rollback on failure:

[switch]
rollback = true
backups = true

items.title: Rollback strategies
items.text: (Documentation in progress)

items.title: Automatic rollback (recommended)
items.text:
[switch]
rollback = true

If migrations fail, fmd automatically reverts to the previous working release.

items.title: Manual rollback
items.text:
When you need to rollback after a successful deployment:

fmd release list mysite.localhost
fmd release switch mysite.localhost release_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS

items.title: Disable rollback (debugging)
items.text:
[switch]
rollback = false

Failed deployments stay on previous release, new release preserved for debugging.

items.title: Backup strategies
items.text: (Documentation in progress)

items.title: Automatic backups
items.text:
[switch]
backups = true
backup_retention = 5

items.title: Manual backups
items.text:
Before major changes:

# Via Frappe Manager
fm backup mysite

items.title: Recovery workflows
items.text: (Documentation in progress)

items.title: Scenario 1: Migration failed
items.text: → Automatic rollback (if enabled)

items.title: Scenario 2: Code bug discovered post-deployment
items.text: → Manual rollback to previous release

items.title: Scenario 3: Data corruption
items.text: → Database restore from backup

items.title: Scenario 4: Complete site failure
items.text: → Disaster recovery procedure

items.title: Release retention
items.text:
(Documentation in progress)

[release]
releases_retain_limit = 7

Old releases are automatically moved to deployment-backup/ directory.
